coredata.dc:title: Novel up-conversion N, S co-doped carbon dots/g-C3N4 photocatalyst for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution under visible and near-infrared light
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Yongpan
Sun, Lixiang
Feng, Haoqiang
Li, Yike
Li, Zhongjun
coredata.dc:description: In photocatalytic splitting water for hydrogen evolution, narrow light response range and fast electron-hole recombination of g-C3N4 (CN) limit its photocatalytic activity. In this article, the N, S co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) with up-converted property were loaded on CN nanosheets by thermal polymerization to obtain NSCDs/CN composite catalyst. Characterization, electrochemical researches and hydrogen evolution tests suggest that the photocatalytic activity of CN is greatly promoted by the introduction of NSCDs. Under visible and near-infrared irradiation, the hydrogen evolution rate is 5033.1 μmol g−1 h−1 of NSCDs-5/CN, which is 8.3 times higher than that of CN. The performance improvement is mainly attributed to the increased specific surface area, elevated hydrophilic surface, increased light absorption and suppressed carrier recombination of CN after the introduction of NSCDs. This work unveils the mechanism of the hydrogen evolution activity improvement in NSCDs-5/CN, and also offers a new prospect in the design of high-performance CN-based photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S co-doped carbon dots
Up-conversion
NSCDs/CN
Near-infrared light
Hydrogen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the N, S co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21671176 and 21001096 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) FT-IR spectra of NSCDs.
(a) The photoluminescence and (b) up-converted emission spectra of NSCDs excited under different excitation wavelengths.
SEM images of (a) CN and (b) NSCDs-5/CN; (c–e) TEM image, HRTEM image and STEM elemental mapping of NSCDs-5/CN.
(a) XRD spectra, (b) FT-IR spectra of the NSCDs/CN composite; (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (d) Pore-size distribution of CN and NSCDs-5/CN.
(a) XPS survey spectra, (b) C 1s and (c) N 1s spectra of CN and NSCDs-5/CN; (d) O 1s spectra of NSCDs-5/CN.
(a) UV–vis DRS of the NSCDs/CN composite; (b) Tauc curves, (c) Valence band XPS pattern and (d) Relative bandgap positions of CN and NSCDs-5/CN.

(a) The properties of H2 production of the NSCDs/CN composite; (b) H2 evolution rate (a. CN, b. NSCDs-1/CN, c. NSCDs-3/CN, d. NSCDs-5/CN, e. NSCDs-8/CN); (c) H2 production rate of the NSCDs-5/CN with different calcination temperature; (d) Recyclability test of the NSCDs-5/CN.
(a) Photoluminescence spectra, (b) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves, (c) Transient photocurrent curves and (d) EIS curves of the CN and NSCDs-5/CN.
The contact angle of (a) CN and (b) NSCDs-5/CN; (c) H2 evolution rate of the CN and NSCDs-5/CN at long wavelengths (λ > 550 nm).
Schematic of possible up-converted mechanism of NSCDs enhanced H2 production in NSCDs-5/CN sample under visible and NIR light.
Percentages of photoinduced charge carrier and fluorescence decay lifetimes of as synthesized materials.

head.ce:title: Novel up-conversion N, S co-doped carbon dots/g-C3N4 photocatalyst for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution under visible and near-infrared light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In photocatalytic splitting water for hydrogen evolution, narrow light response range and fast electron-hole recombination of g-C3N4 (CN) limit its photocatalytic activity. In this article, the N, S co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) with up-converted property were loaded on CN nanosheets by thermal polymerization to obtain NSCDs/CN composite catalyst. Characterization, electrochemical researches and hydrogen evolution tests suggest that the photocatalytic activity of CN is greatly promoted by the introduction of NSCDs. Under visible and near-infrared irradiation, the hydrogen evolution rate is 5033.1 μmol g−1 h−1 of NSCDs-5/CN, which is 8.3 times higher than that of CN. The performance improvement is mainly attributed to the increased specific surface area, elevated hydrophilic surface, increased light absorption and suppressed carrier recombination of CN after the introduction of NSCDs. This work unveils the mechanism of the hydrogen evolution activity improvement in NSCDs-5/CN, and also offers a new prospect in the design of high-performance CN-based photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of global economy and growth of social population have accelerated the consumption of non-renewable energy sources and caused serious environmental issue. The resulting energy shortage and environmental degradation have gravely menaced the sustainable development of human society [1,2]. Hence, the exploitation and utilization of new energy with low-cost, clean and renewable in an environmentally sustainable way is an ideal pathway to cope with the environmental problem and energy crisis [3,4]. So far, photocatalytic hydrogen production technology is considered to be one of the most effective and promising ways to deal with these problems [5,6]. To achieve this result, the primary task is to research semiconductor catalytic materials with high catalytic performance.

As a photocatalyst responsive to visible light, graphitic carbon nitride (CN) has shown potential application for photocatalytic water-splitting thanks to its facile synthesis, nontoxicity, suitable band structure, low cost and good stability, etc [7,8]. Unfortunately, the development of CN is limited by two factors: the visible light response less than 460 nm results in its limited utilization of light in the solar spectrum and the rapid recombination of photo-excited carriers leads to its poor catalytic efficiency in photocatalysis hydrogen evolution [9,10]. It is well-known that sunlight is composed of 52% near-infrared (NIR) light, 43% visible light and 4% ultraviolet light. Therefore, to solve the above defects of CN, incorporating with other semiconductors to form heterojunction has been proved to be an effective pathway, as it can accelerate the separation of photo-excited carriers, promote the absorption of visible light and even near-infrared light [11,12].

As a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials, Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention due to its nontoxicity, water solubility, excellent electrical conductivity and quantum size effect [13,14]. Besides, some CDs also possess the unique property of up-converted photoluminescence. That is to say, the CDs can be excited by the long wavelengths light of 500–900 nm and emit short wavelengths light of 400–550 nm, thereby increasing light absorption and utilization of sunlight [15]. Thanks to these physicochemical properties, CDs have drawn tremendous attention and utilized in many fields, such as imaging [16], sensing [17,18], energy storage [19], photodynamic antimicrobial therapy [20], electro- and photocatalysis [21,22]. In the field of photocatalysis, CDs are often combined with some inorganic semiconductor materials to enhance the photocatalytic properties of semiconductors. Nowadays, many CDs-based hybrids such as CDs/Ag3PO4 [23], CDs/Bi2MoO6 [24], Fe2O3/CDs [25], CDs/TiO2 [26], CDs/BiOCl [27] and CDs/Bi2WO6 [28] have been reported and displayed the obviously enhanced catalytic efficiencies under visible light irradiation. The integration of CDs with above-mentioned inorganic semiconductor has been indicated the enhancement of light response and the suppression of photo-excited charge carriers recombination simultaneously, therefore the photocatalytic performance of composite catalysts can be improved. Considering such outstanding properties of CDs and the limitations of the CN catalyst, we infer that the combination of CDs and CN may be an ideal way to build efficient and stable complex photocatalytic systems.

Herein, the N, S co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) with up-converted photoluminescence were constructed via a simply hydrothermal treatment. Then, an in-situ calcination strategy was utilized to load NSCDs on CN. The physical and chemical properties of as-formed NSCDs/CN composite were investigated in detail. Their photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance were tested under visible and NIR light and the photocatalytic mechanism was discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the N, S co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NSCDs were synthesized according to previous reported work [29]. In general, 9.00 mL ethylene glycol, 4.75 mL deionized water and 6.25 mL HNO3 (68%) were placed in a 25 mL Teflon autoclave. Then, 1.5 g of l-cysteine was dissolved in the above as-prepared solution. The aqueous mixture was stirred for 10 min and then heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged to remove undispersed solids. The supernatant was then dialyzed and freeze-dried to obtain NSCDs sample.

A certain amount of NSCDs sample and 30.0 g of urea were added in 50 mL of water. The dispersed solution was stirred for 2 h and then vaporized at 70 °C. The gained solid powder was heated in muffle furnace at 500 °C for 2 h at 3 °C min−1. The resultant NSCDs/CN was collected and milled into powders for further use. Different NSCDs/CN samples were obtained by changing the amount of NSCDs and marked as NSCDs-1/CN (1 wt%), NSCDs-3/CN (3 wt%), NSCDs-5/CN (5 wt%), and NSCDs-8/CN (8 wt%), respectively. The g-C3N4 nanosheets (CN) were gained by the similar pathway without the addition of NSCDs.

UV–vis DRS was measured on Cary 5000 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra (XPS) and Valence band XPS was performed on Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and Fourier transform-infrared spectra (FTIR) were gained on Hitachi F4600 spectrophotometer and Scientific Nicolet iS5 spectrometer. The N2 sorption isotherm was recorded by gas absorption analyzer (Belsorp Max II). The fluorescence decay lifetimes were measured by Steady State and Transient State Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh, FLS 980). Standard Type Contact Angle Meter (JY-82B Kruss DSA) was used to study hydrophilicity of catalyst. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) was carried out using PAN alytical X'Pert PRO. The morphology and size of catalyst were gained via Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, Sigma500) and Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20S).

The photocatalytic H2 evolution of catalysts was performed using an online photocatalytic test system equipped with a Pyrex glass reactor. In detail, 0.05 g of catalyst was added in triethanolamine solution (10 vol %, 100 mL). Subsequently, 3% Pt was photodeposited onto the samples surface by adding an appropriate amount of H2PtCl6·6H2O. The light source is 300 W xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm), and the evolved H2 gas was monitored as a function of time using a gas chromatograph. The apparent quantum yield (AQY) of NSCDs-5/CN was calculated according to the following formula [30]:
A Q Y =
n u m b e r
o f
h y d r o g e n
m o l e c u l e s × 2
n u m b e r
o f
i n c i d e n t
p h o t o n s
× 100 %

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent (I-t) were performed on CHI660E (three-electrode cell: photocatalyst, platinum sheet, Ag/AgCl). The electrolyte is Na2SO4 (0.5 M) and the light source is 100 W xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm).

The TEM image of NSCDs in Fig. 1 a displays as-prepared NSCDs with a quantum size (about 1–3 nm) distribution are highly monodispersed, which is consistent with previous report [29]. In order to determine the bond structure of NSCDs, FT-IR spectra was measured and given in Fig. 1b. The two absorption peaks at 1088 and 3430 cm−1 stand for the C–O bond and O–H component stretching vibrations of glycol units in NSCDs sample, respectively [31]. The peak at 1205 cm−1 stands for the C–S and C–N stretching vibrations, originating from l-cysteine [29]. Besides, other absorption peaks locate at 1630, 1730, 2923 and 2966 cm−1 can be ascribed to N–H, –CO of carbonyl groups, alkyl chain groups in the NSCDs.

To obtain more optical information, photoluminescence (PL) of the NSCDs was measured in different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the PL peak shifts from 420 nm to 490 nm, when the wavelength is excited from 320 to 420 nm. This shows that it has excitation wavelength-dependent PL spectra [32,33]. In addition, the strongest PL peak appears under an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. In Fig. 2b, clearly, the NSCDs exhibit obvious up-converted photoluminescence behavior. When the NSCDs are excited by long-wavelength light, from 700 nm to 800 nm (near NIR light region), the up-converted emissions are remarkably located at visible light wavelengths. That is to say, the NSCDs can absorb near-infrared light and then emit visible light. The above results demonstrate that NSCDs can enhance photocatalytic efficiency by converting near-infrared wavelengths to visible light, which enables it to be utilized as powerful energy transfer components in the design of photocatalysts.

The morphology and structure features of CN and NSCDs-5/CN were obtained through SEM and TEM. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the pristine CN exhibits sheet-like structure, which is retained well after the introduction of NSCDs (Fig. 3b). In enlarged TEM image of NSCDs-5/CN (Fig. 3c), some quantum dots can be clearly observed on the CN surface, indicating that NSCDs were successfully introduced into CN. In Fig. 3d, the distinct lattice fringe with an interplanar spacing of 0.26 nm is attributed to (100) plane of graphite. The EDX elemental mapping shown in Fig. 3e reveals the C, N, O, S located homogeneously in the obtained NSCDs-5/CN catalyst, proving the NSCDs are evenly distributed on the CN surface.

XRD was tested to study the crystal structures of CN and NSCDs/CN composites. In Fig. 4 a, all catalysts show the similar XRD patterns. Two typical peaks at 13.1° and 27.9° can be attributed to the (100) and (002) planes of CN (JCPDS 87-1526), respectively. However, the intensities of the two diffractive peaks decrease compared with CN, which is caused by the hindering of order thermopolymerization of tri-s-triazine units and the stacking of CN in NSCDs-5/CN sample due to the introduction of NSCDs, resulting in the decrease of CN crystallization degree [34,35]. Moreover, no new peak is displayed in the patterns of NSCDs/CN composites compared with CN, revealing that the introduction of NSCDs makes no difference in the morphology of CN [36]. The plentiful chemical bond information of NSCDs/CN composites and CN was studied by FT-IR and displayed in Fig. 4b, the absorption bands locate between 3650 and 3000 cm−1 can be ascribed to the N–H groups and O–H stretching modes. The 1200–1650 cm−1 intense bands stand for the stretching vibration modes of the CN heterocycle [37]. Meanwhile, we can observe an clear absorption band at 804 cm−1 corresponding to the characteristic breathing mode of tri-s-triazine units [38]. In addition, FT-IR spectra of all samples shows the same characteristic vibration modes, demonstrating the morphology of CN is basically unaltered, in spite of the integration of NSCDs. However, the vibration frequency gradually decrease as the increase of NSCDs content, which might be taken as an indicator that CDs have been coupled with CN successfully [39,40]. The surface area and pore size of the samples were tested and the analysis results are shown in Fig. 4c–d. All materials exhibit IV-type isotherms with visible H3 hysteresis loop, which indicate the existence of meso-pores (Fig. 4c) [41]. The SBET of NSCDs-5/CN is 99.9 m2 g−1, which is larger than that of CN (71.4 m2 g−1). The larger SBET of NSCDs-5/CN results from the enriched pore structure, which is beneficial to exposing more catalytic active sites [42]. Furthermore, the Barret–Joyner–Halenda pore-size distribution in Fig. 4d shows that both the microporous and mesoporous characteristics of the CN and NSCDs-5/CN.

XPS analysis was conducted to investigate the element chemical interaction of catalysts. The survey spectra in Fig. 5 a mainly exhibits three peaks at 283.6 (C 1s), 397.7 (N 1s) and 530.0 eV (O 1s). However, the S 2s and S 2p could not be detected in the NSCDs-5/CN, which may be caused by the small loading amount of NSCDs. In Fig. 5b, the C 1s spectra of CN displays two major peaks at 284.2 and 287.6, which attributed to the adventitious hydrocarbons and NC–N units in the framework of CN, respectively [43]. By contrast, a new peak appears at 285.9 eV over C 1s of NSCDs-5/CN assigns to the oxygen-containing functional groups (C–OH) from NSCDs, confirming that NSCDs are successfully introduced into CN [23]. Fig. 5c gives the N 1s spectra of two samples, and the spectra can be fitted into four peaks with 398.1 (CN–C bonds, N1), 399.4 (N-(C)3, N2), 400.3 (N–H group, N3) and 403.9 eV (excitation N, N4) [44]. Interestingly, the peak-area ratio of N1/N2 for NSCDs-5/CN (7.7) is found to be higher than that of CN (6.0), suggesting the increase of the CN–C groups. This result verifies the coalescence of amide bonds between CN and NSCDs [45]. Furthermore, the peak at 531.8 eV suggests the existence of CO groups in NSCDs-5/CN (Fig. 5d) [46].

The light response range is a key factor in photocatalysis. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the absorption band edge of CN is located at ∼450 nm (main absorption UV and part visible light), however, when hybridized with NSCDs, the absorption band of NSCDs/CN complex is red-shifted and light absorption region expanded from part visible to NIR light, which is attributed to the excellent light capture capability of NSCDs [47]. This result also reveals the successful coupling of NSCDs and CN. According to the absorption spectra, the Tauc curves were shown in Fig. 6b, based on which the bandgap of CN and NSCDs-5/CN are calculated to be 2.76, 2.28 eV, respectively. The bandgap of CN is slightly narrower after combining with NSCDs, which is useful for enhancing the utilization of visible light and enhancing the photocatalytic reactions for hydrogen evolution [48]. In addition, the valence band position of the CN and NSCDs-5/CN was further estimated and shown in Fig. 6c, the valence band (VB) positions of CN and NSCDs-5/CN are both 1.6 V. Accordingly, Fig. 6d schematically describes the relative bandgap positions of two samples. It can be found that the VB positions of CN and NSCDs-5/CN are kept constant, while the slight shifts are observed in conduction band (CB) position. Based on previously reported literature, the work function of CN is smaller than that of NSCDs [40,49]. Therefore, for NSCDs-5/CN, when NSCDs and CN intimately contact, the electrons may migrate from CN to NSCDs. The accumulation of electrons on NSCDs will cause the CB potential of NSCDs-5/CN down-shifted to a more positive potential, thereby achieving Fermi level equilibrium between them [50].

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activities of CN and NSCDs-5/CN nanocomposites were investigated under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, all the NSCDs/CN nanocomposites display significantly increased photocatalytic activities compared with the CN in the photocatalytic reaction. The H2 production of the NSCDs-5/CN can be up to 5033.1 μmol g−1 h−1, which is 8.3 times than that of CN (607.5 μmol g−1 h−1), benefiting from the up-converted photoluminescence behavior of NSCDs and efficient photo-excited carrier separation of NSCDs-5/CN material [51]. The apparent quantum yield (AQY) of NSCDs-5/CN material at λ = 420 nm was measured to be 26.3%. Compared with previously reported literature, the photocatalytic efficiency of the NSCDs-5/CN is higher than some CN-based photocatalysts (Table S1). Furthermore, the effect of calcination temperature on the catalytic performance of catalyst was investigated. Some samples with different calcination temperature (300, 400 and 600 °C) were synthesized, and investigated the HER performance of these samples (See Fig. 7c). It is found that the NSCDs/CN prepared at 500 °C exhibits the best hydrogen production activities. The H2 evolution rates of samples with different calcination temperature are 0, 718.3, 5033.1, and 3152.2 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively. The above result demonstrates that the performance of catalyst is affected by calcination temperature. Catalytic stability is another vital parameter for evaluating the real application of photocatalyst material. Thus, the stability of NSCDs-5/CN sample was carried out in a six-cycle measurement. As shown in Fig. 7d, there is no obvious performance decrease compared with the first cycling test, revealing the superior cycle ability. Furthermore, the SEM image (Fig. S3), XRD and XPS spectra (Fig. S4) of the recovered NSCDs-5/CN after cycling exhibit that there is no obvious changes in the morphology, crystal structure and bond property compared to the original sample, which confirms that the photocatalyst possesses superior stability under consecutive irradiation.

The carrier recombination process and charge-carrier transfer behavior can be further proven by photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy, time-resolved photoluminescence, transient photocurrent responses and EIS. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, the PL spectrum exhibits a PL peak at 455 nm at 370 nm excitation, which originates from the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes in CN. After being decorated with NSCDs, the NSCDs-5/CN shows obvious PL quenching phenomenon compared with CN sample, indicating that the coupling of NSCDs with CN nanosheets can facilitate the separate of electrons and holes. In Fig. 8b and Table 1 , the nanosecond-level time-resolved fluorescence decay experiment results show that there is a more noticeably decreased lifetime for the NSCDs-5/CN (4.1 ns) sample compared to the CN (9.7 ns). This proves that recombination of photo-excited carriers is effectively suppressed and the separation of photo-generated carriers is greatly promoted in the NSCDs-5/CN material [52]. The similar results are also presented in Fig. 8c. We can obviously observe that the transient photocurrent of NSCDs-5/CN is higher than that of CN upon each illumination, which also indicates that NSCDs-5/CN possesses much higher photo-generated carriers transfer ability [53]. Moreover, the EIS results of CN and NSCDs-5/CN are presented in Fig. 8d. The arc radius of the NSCDs-5/CN is significantly smaller compared with CN, indicating that the NSCDs-5/CN are more effective in accelerating the interfacial charge transmit, which also corresponds to the results of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [54].

Besides, hydrophilicity of catalyst is also a vital factor to affect the catalytic performance. Normally, the more hydrophilic the catalyst surface, the smaller the contact resistance between the catalyst and water. So, to verify the hydrophilicity of the NSCDs-5/CN, we conducted contact angle experiment. In Fig. 9 a and b, the CN possesses a high average contact angle of 41.11°, while a decreasing average contact angle of 33° is obviously appeared for NSCDs-5/CN material due to the introduction of NSCDs with high hydrophilic groups. This indicates better hydrophilic surface of NSCDs-5/CN than that of CN, which can enable the sufficient contact of the catalyst with water and thus achieve better photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity [55]. For the effect of up-converted photoluminescence property of the NSCDs, we also performed hydrogen evolution experiments at long wavelengths using a 550 nm cut-off filter. The results in Fig. 9c show that the H2 evolution of CN is zero, while 262.6 μmol g−1 h−1 was detected on hybrid sample NSCDs-5/CN. These results confirm the above-mentioned assertion that the carbon dots can convert NIR light to the light of visible region for CN absorption, thereby increasing the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

Based on the overall experimental data, the reasons for the improved photocatalytic H2 generation and stability of NSCDs-5/CN may be as follows: Firstly, the NSCDs-5/CN possesses larger surface area than CN, which will be able to expose more active sites for photocatalytic H2 evolution. Secondly, the up-converted PL property of the NSCDs can enhance the light absorption range of hybrid materials. Thirdly, as an electron collector, the higher conductivity of carbon dots can speed up charge transport and enhance the separation of photogenerated carriers. Fourth, the excellent water solubility of NSCDs can boost the hydrophilicity of CN and the contact with water in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction, leading to more active sites and better photocatalytic activity.

In the end, based on the above results and previous reports, a possible mechanism was proposed and illustrated in Scheme 1 . Under light irradiation, the visible light with a wavelength <460 nm could be absorbed directly by the CN. Meanwhile, other visible light and NIR with a wavelength >460 nm can be converted to <460 light for CN absorption because of the up-converted photoluminescence property of the NSCDs. After CN absorbs light, electron-hole pairs were formed. Owing to carbon dots can as an electron collector, the electrons on CN can easily transmit to NSCDs, thereby suppressing electrons and holes recombination. Then, the electrons are further transferred to Pt to reduce H+ into H2. Meanwhile, the holes in the VB position of CN were consumed by TEOA. Thus, the hydrogen production activity of NSCDs-5/CN sample was effectively improved thanks to the efficient photo-excited carrier separation.

In summary, a new photocatalyst NSCDs-5/CN was successful obtained through thermal polymerization of urea and NSCDs. The introduction of NSCDs has significant effect on improving the photocatalytic activity of catalyst. The up-converted properties of NSCDs can increase the utilization of light. Using excellent water solubility NSCDs as the electron collector can inhibit the recombination of photo-excited carriers and improve the hydrophilicity of CN. Benefiting from these advantages, under visible and near-infrared light, the H2 evolution of NSCDs-5/CN is as high as 5033.1 μmol g−1 h−1, which is about 8.3 times higher than that of CN. In addition, the NSCDs-5/CN also exhibits excellent recycle stability. This work offers a detailed insight into the possible improvement of hydrogen evolution activity through the introduction of NSCDs with up-converted features, which may stimulate people for further mechanism study of carbon dots-based material systems with up-converted features.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21671176 and 21001096).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2022.11.179.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel nonmetal intercalated high crystalline g-C3N4 photocatalyst for efficiency enhanced H2 evolution
Int J Hydrogen Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Hu, J. Shu, L. Xu, C. Liu, Q. Feng, A novel nonmetal intercalated high crystalline g-C3N4 photocatalyst for efficiency enhanced H2 evolution, Int J Hydrogen Energy, 47 (2022) 11841-11852.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of g-C3N4/WO3-carbon microsphere composites for photocatalytic hydrogen production
Int J Hydrogen Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Sun, Y. Zhou, T. Yu, J. Wang, Synthesis of g-C3N4/WO3-carbon microsphere composites for photocatalytic hydrogen production, Int J Hydrogen Energy, 47 (2022) 10261-10276.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-situ synthesis of ternary heterojunctions via g-C3N4 coupling with noble-metal-free NiS and CdS with efficient visible-light-induced photocatalytic H2 evolution and mechanism insight
Int J Hydrogen Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wu, D. Lu, K.K. Kondamareddy, W. Ho, Q. Wang, Y. Zhang, Y. Zeng, B. Zhang, L. Xie, B. Zhao, Z. Wang, H. Hao, H. Fan, H. Wang, In-situ synthesis of ternary heterojunctions via g-C3N4 coupling with noble-metal-free NiS and CdS with efficient visible-light-induced photocatalytic H2 evolution and mechanism insight, Int J Hydrogen Energy, 47 (2022) 14063-14076.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 1D/2D carbon-doped nanowire/ultra-thin nanosheet g-C3N4 isotype heterojunction for effective and durable photocatalytic H2 evolution
Int J Hydrogen Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yang, Z. Wang, J. Zhao, X. Sun, R. Wang, G. Liao, X. Jia, 1D/2D carbon-doped nanowire/ultra-thin nanosheet g-C3N4 isotype heterojunction for effective and durable photocatalytic H2 evolution, Int J Hydrogen Energy, 46 (2021) 25436-25447.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ZnIn2S4 decorated Co-doped NH2-MIL-53(Fe) nanocomposites for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production
